---
title: "Pre-Homework: Review & Software Check"
subtitle: "Due Before Day 1"
date: "Estimated time: 1.5 hours"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
---

::: {.callout-tip icon=true}
## Do this assignment in Colab

The interactive version with answer cells lives in a Colab notebook:
[**Open Pre-Homework in Colab**](https://colab.research.google.com/github/pflahert/hd-stats-workshop/blob/main/notebooks/pre-homework.ipynb)

This page is the readable web reference. Type your answers in the Colab notebook (`File → Save a copy in Drive` to keep your work).
:::

## Purpose

This pre-homework ensures everyone starts Day 1 with a shared foundation in hypothesis testing and $p$-values. It also introduces the multiple testing problem and gives you practice working *conversationally* with an AI assistant.

**Learning Outcomes**: LO1, LO3

::: {.callout-note}
## How this notebook uses AI

This notebook integrates AI at several steps, not just at the end. Two kinds of AI exercises appear:

- **Single-shot critiques** — you prompt, paste the response, and evaluate it against a named checklist (Pass / Fail / Partial + Notes).
- **Multi-turn conversations** — you prompt, critique the response, write a follow-up prompt that addresses your critique, paste the refined response, and write a final assessment. *At least one multi-turn exercise per notebook is required.* In this notebook, Q3.1 is the multi-turn exercise — Turns 2 and 3 must be sent in the same chat session so the AI can revise its earlier answer.

Use any AI assistant (the [UMass campus GenAI platform](https://www.umass.edu/it/genai/platform), Claude, ChatGPT, or another). Note which AI you used for each prompt — different models behave differently, and the differences are part of the lesson.
:::

---

## Part 1: Review of Hypothesis Testing (30 min)

### Reading

Review the basics of hypothesis testing from your introductory statistics course or from this free reference:

- *Computational and Inferential Thinking* (Berkeley Data 8), Ch. 11 — [Testing Hypotheses](https://inferentialthinking.com/chapters/11/Testing_Hypotheses.html)

### Questions

**Q1.1**: In your own words, define: (a) Null hypothesis ($H_0$), (b) Alternative hypothesis ($H_1$), (c) $p$-value, (d) **False positive** — rejecting $H_0$ when $H_0$ is actually true (also called a *Type I error*), (e) **False negative** — failing to reject $H_0$ when $H_1$ is actually true (also called a *Type II error*).

**Q1.2**: A researcher tests whether a new drug reduces blood pressure. They report $p = 0.03$.

(a) State the null and alternative hypotheses.
(b) At significance level $\alpha = 0.05$, do they reject $H_0$?
(c) The $p$-value is NOT the probability that $H_0$ is true. Explain why not, in one or two sentences.

**Q1.3**: If you perform a test at $\alpha = 0.05$ and the null hypothesis is actually true, what is the probability of a **false positive**?\* If you perform 20 such independent tests (all with true nulls), what is the probability of getting at least one false positive?

\* *Exactly $\alpha$ for tests with continuous null distributions (the case here for a $t$-test on continuous data); $\le \alpha$ for discrete tests.*

*Hint*: $P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - P(\text{none}) = 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{20}$.

**Q1.4 — AI simulation check.** Ask an AI assistant to verify Q1.3 by Monte Carlo simulation. Use this prompt:

> "Reply with a single Python code block (no prose, no .docx file — just runnable code I can paste into a Colab cell). Verify by Monte Carlo simulation: if I run 20 independent two-sample Welch $t$-tests (`scipy.stats.ttest_ind(..., equal_var=False)`) on data drawn from the same Normal distribution (so the null is true for all 20), what is the probability that at least one test produces a $p$-value below 0.05? Use 10,000 replicates with `numpy.random.seed(2026)`. Compare the simulation estimate to the analytical formula $1 - (1-0.05)^{20}$ and print both, labeled. Use `numpy` and `scipy.stats` only."

Paste the AI's code in the Colab notebook and run it. In the notebook, complete a critique table (Pass / Fail / Partial + Notes): did the AI set the seed? use 10,000 replicates? call `ttest_ind` with `equal_var=False` (Welch)? print both numbers? are they within ~1% of each other?

---

## Part 2: The Multiple Testing Problem (20 min)

### A thought experiment

Imagine you are a genomics researcher. You have gene expression data for 10,000 genes measured on 40 patients (20 with disease, 20 healthy controls). You run a $t$-test for each gene to see if its expression differs between the two groups.

**Q2.1**: If none of the 10,000 genes are truly associated with the disease, how many genes would you expect to have $p < 0.05$ by chance alone?

**Q2.2**: Now suppose 100 of the 10,000 genes are truly associated, and you have 80% power to detect them (so you correctly reject $H_0$ for $\approx 80$ of those 100). The remaining 9,900 null genes still produce approximately $9{,}900 \times 0.05 \approx 495$ false positives. If you report all genes with $p < 0.05$ as "significant," roughly what fraction of your reported genes would be false positives? *Hint*: total reported $\approx 80 + 495 = 575$; false positives $\approx 495$.

**Q2.3**: Suggest at least one approach to deal with this problem. You don't need to know the formal name — just describe the idea intuitively.

---

## Part 3: Working with AI Tools (30 min)

### Q3.1 — Multi-turn: test the AI on something you know

*This is the multi-turn AI exercise required for this notebook.* **Three turns of model output in the same chat session** so the AI can build on (and revise) its earlier answer — *don't start a fresh conversation between turns*.

**Turn 1 — initial prompt.** Ask the AI:

> "What is a $p$-value? Explain it in two sentences."

In the Colab notebook, paste the response and complete a Pass / Fail / Partial critique against five classic mistakes (does it say "$P(H_0\text{ true})$"? "$P(\text{chance})$"? does it condition on $H_0$ explicitly? does it use the *as-extreme-or-more-extreme* phrasing? does it disambiguate one-sided vs two-sided?).

**Turn 2 — follow-up prompt that addresses your critique.** *In the same chat session*, send a prompt that names the specific failure you flagged in your own words. Example: *"Your definition implied that the $p$-value is the probability that $H_0$ is true. That's a common mistake. Rewrite the definition correctly: it should be a probability under the null, of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed. Two sentences again."*

**Turn 3 — make it usable.** In the same chat session, ask the AI to produce a one-sentence version you could paste into a methods paragraph for a paper, with conditioning on $H_0$ and "as extreme or more extreme" included.

Final assessment: did the Turn-3 sentence actually correct the issues from Turn 1, or did the AI quietly reintroduce the original error?

**Q3.2** — Ask the AI a harder question (single-shot):

> "Reply in two sentences only (plain text, no .docx file). I ran 10,000 $t$-tests on gene expression data and found 600 genes with $p < 0.05$. I corrected with Bonferroni and now only 15 genes are significant. A reviewer says I should use FDR instead of Bonferroni. Explain why the reviewer might be right."

In the Colab notebook, paste the response and complete a Pass / Fail / Partial critique: did the AI distinguish FWER from FDR correctly? did it explain why FDR is more appropriate for discovery (controls a *fraction*, not a probability)? did it avoid the common error "FDR is the probability that a finding is false"? did it stay within two sentences?

**Q3.3** — Ask the AI to write code (paste-ready, with Python format directive and seeding):

> "Reply with a single Python code block (no prose, no .docx file — just runnable code I can paste into a Colab cell). Set `rng = numpy.random.default_rng(2026)`. Use `rng` to: (1) generate 1000 $p$-values from a Uniform(0, 1) distribution and plot their histogram; (2) generate 1000 $p$-values where 950 are Uniform(0, 1) and 50 are draws from a Beta(0.1, 1) distribution via `scipy.stats.beta.rvs(a=0.1, b=1, size=50, random_state=rng)`. Plot the two histograms side by side on a single matplotlib figure with shared x-axis. Label axes, add titles, and use 50 bins each. Use `numpy`, `scipy.stats`, and `matplotlib.pyplot` only."

In the Colab notebook, paste the AI-generated code, run it, and complete a Pass / Fail / Partial critique: did the AI use `default_rng(2026)`? produce *two* histograms? use the correct 950 + 50 mixture proportions? call `scipy.stats.beta.rvs(a=0.1, b=1, size=50, random_state=rng)` (a *callable* on the frozen distribution, not just an instantiation)? label axes?

---

## Part 4: Software Check (10 min)

The workshop runs entirely in Python via Google Colab — **you do not need R for any part of this workshop**. The notebook's Python software-check cell confirms that the required packages are available; in Colab almost everything is preinstalled.

Verify you have access to the [**UMass campus GenAI platform**](https://www.umass.edu/it/genai/platform) (or Claude / ChatGPT) and can paste code into it and receive responses. The labs and the homework make heavy use of AI prompts, and you should know which platform you'll use before Day 1.

---

## Submission

Submit your completed `pre-homework.ipynb` (`File → Download → Download .ipynb` in Colab) to the workshop Harmony site before the start of Day 1. We will briefly discuss the answers at the start of Lecture 1.
